notes: Chef review wave 5. Backstage source notes only; do not expose source notes on public recipe pages.

recipes.review_summary: Alloco was directionally correct, but the pantry details were too thin for a cookable page. The corrected recipe keeps ripe plantain, hot oil, piment, onion, tomato, and salt front and center.
recommended_changes: Add salt and a brighter piment sauce.
Keep ripe plantains, not green plantains.
Serve as street food with grilled fish, eggs, or piment.
recipes.official_source_status: No single official recipe standard found. Tourism and Ivorian food sources agree that alloco is ripe fried plantain commonly eaten with piment, onions, fish, or eggs.

recipe.description: Ivorian fried ripe plantain served hot with onion-tomato piment, a street-food plate that works as snack or side.
recipe.story: Alloco should taste sweet, salty, and hot. Use plantains with black spots, fry them in batches, and do not skip the piment.
recipe.best_served_with: Grilled fish, boiled eggs, roasted peanuts, or extra piment
recipe.regional_variations: Some sellers serve alloco with fried fish, hard-boiled eggs, peanuts, or a raw onion and chile relish.

replace_steps.title: Slice plantain
replace_steps.instruction: Peel the ripe plantains and slice them diagonally about 1 cm thick. Sprinkle lightly with salt.

replace_steps.title: Fry in batches
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a deep pan. Fry plantain slices in batches until both sides are deep golden, then drain on paper.

replace_steps.title: Make piment
replace_steps.instruction: Mix tomato, onion, scotch bonnet, garlic if using, salt, and a squeeze of lime. Keep it fresh and sharp.

replace_steps.title: Serve hot
replace_steps.instruction: Pile alloco onto a platter and serve with piment on the side or spooned over the top.

recipes.review_summary: The old page had the right idea but missed several ingredients used in its own instructions. The corrected version gives the fish marinade, attiéké steaming, onion-tomato garnish, and piment enough detail to cook.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, ginger, lime, salt, pepper, and optional mustard to the fish marinade.
Keep raw onion, tomato, cucumber, and piment as the bright plate finish.
Steam and fluff the attiéké rather than treating it like dry couscous.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Ivorian and Francophone recipe sources consistently pair attiéké with grilled or fried fish, onion-tomato garnish, and piment.

sources.title: NKOSI - Recette Attiéké au Poisson Ivoirien

recipe.description: Fermented cassava attiéké served with grilled fish, onion-tomato salad, cucumber, lime, and hot Ivorian piment.
recipe.story: The plate works because every part has a job: smoky fish, fluffy attiéké, fresh onion and tomato, and a hot piment that wakes everything up.
recipe.best_served_with: Alloco, extra piment, sliced cucumber, and cold ginger juice
recipe.regional_variations: The fish may be grilled, fried, or oven-roasted; the attiéké is commonly finished with a little oil and served with raw garnish.

replace_steps.title: Marinate fish
replace_steps.instruction: Rub fish with oil, garlic, ginger, mustard if using, lime juice, salt, and pepper. Rest while the grill heats.

replace_steps.title: Grill fish
replace_steps.instruction: Grill or roast the fish until the skin is crisp and the flesh flakes easily, turning once.

replace_steps.title: Steam attiéké
replace_steps.instruction: Steam the attiéké until hot and fluffy. Break up clumps with a fork and drizzle with a little oil.

replace_steps.title: Prepare garnish
replace_steps.instruction: Toss onion, tomato, cucumber if using, salt, lime, and a little piment. Keep some piment separate for the table.

replace_steps.title: Plate
replace_steps.instruction: Serve the attiéké with grilled fish, fresh garnish, and piment on the side.

recipes.review_summary: The old pilau had the right ingredients but the sequence risked tough beef. The corrected method cooks the beef stock first, then builds the browned onion and spice base before steaming the rice.
recommended_changes: Simmer the beef first and reserve the stock.
Brown onions deeply before blooming pilau masala.
Serve with kachumbari to cut through the rich rice.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan cooking sources agree on rice, beef or meat stock, browned onions, garlic, ginger, and pilau spices.

sources.title: My African Food Map - Kenya Pilau

recipe.description: Kenyan beef pilau with browned onions, garlic, ginger, whole spices, pilau masala, and rice steamed in rich stock.
recipe.story: Good pilau starts before the rice. Build a proper beef stock, brown the onions patiently, and let the rice steam without too much stirring.
recipe.best_served_with: Kachumbari, lemon wedges, mango pickle, or plain cabbage salad
recipe.regional_variations: Coastal pilau leans aromatic and spice-forward; some Kenyan homes add potatoes, tomatoes, or extra chile.

replace_steps.title: Cook beef stock
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer beef with half the garlic, half the ginger, salt, pepper, and enough water to cover until nearly tender. Reserve the meat and stock.

replace_steps.title: Brown onions
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a heavy pot and cook onions slowly until deep golden brown.

replace_steps.title: Bloom spices
replace_steps.instruction: Add remaining garlic and ginger, pilau masala, cumin, cardamom, cinnamon, and cloves. Stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Coat rice
replace_steps.instruction: Return beef to the pot. Add drained rice and stir gently until each grain is coated in the spice oil.

replace_steps.title: Steam pilau
replace_steps.instruction: Pour in hot stock, adjust salt, bring to a boil, then cover tightly and cook on the lowest heat until the rice absorbs the liquid.

replace_steps.title: Rest and fluff
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered off heat, then fluff from the edges inward. Serve with kachumbari.

recipes.review_summary: The old nyama choma was tasty but read more like a heavy marinade than the Kenyan classic. The corrected version keeps salt, smoke, optional lemon, and kachumbari as the core.
recommended_changes: Use goat or beef and keep seasoning restrained.
Make kachumbari part of the plate.
Remove halal as a diet tag because the recipe cannot guarantee sourcing.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan and cultural sources consistently describe nyama choma as grilled goat or beef with ugali and kachumbari.

sources.title: Smithsonian Folklife Festival - Nyama Choma with Kachumbari and Ugali
The Domestic Man - Simple Kenyan Grilled Meat
Jikoni Secrets - How to Cook Nyama Choma

recipe.description: Kenyan charcoal-grilled goat or beef, salted simply, roasted slowly, chopped hot, and served with ugali and kachumbari.
recipe.story: Nyama choma is about good meat, patient fire, and a fresh table. Keep the seasoning simple enough for the smoke to speak.
recipe.best_served_with: Ugali, kachumbari, sukuma wiki, and pili pili
recipe.regional_variations: Some cooks add garlic, ginger, paprika, or lemon, but the classic Kenyan approach is salt, good meat, and charcoal.

replace_steps.title: Salt the meat
replace_steps.instruction: Rub meat with salt, pepper if using, and a little lemon or oil if you like. Let it sit while the fire settles.

replace_steps.title: Prepare charcoal
replace_steps.instruction: Build a charcoal fire and wait until the coals are glowing and ashed over. Set up a medium heat zone.

replace_steps.title: Grill slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Grill the meat slowly, turning often, until browned outside and cooked through near the bone.

replace_steps.title: Make kachumbari
replace_steps.instruction: Toss tomatoes, onion, chile if using, cilantro, lime juice, and salt. Keep it crisp and fresh.

replace_steps.title: Rest and chop
replace_steps.instruction: Rest the meat briefly, then chop into pieces and serve with kachumbari and ugali.

recipes.review_summary: The existing ugali and sukuma wiki page was solid. This pass mostly tightens ratios, sides, and language so the greens stay bright and the ugali stays firm.
recommended_changes: Keep maize flour, water, and salt as the ugali base.
Keep sukuma wiki as collards or kale with onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
Cook greens briefly enough to stay green.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Kenyan food sources consistently describe ugali as stiff maize porridge and sukuma wiki as collard greens cooked with onion and tomato.

sources.title: Learn About Kenya - Ugali with Sukuma Wiki

recipe.description: Kenyan firm maize ugali served with sukuma wiki, collard greens quickly cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
recipe.story: Ugali gives the plate its strength; sukuma wiki gives it life. Stir the ugali hard, then cook the greens quickly so they stay fresh.
recipe.best_served_with: Nyama choma, beef stew, beans, or kachumbari
recipe.regional_variations: Some homes keep sukuma wiki very plain with onion and salt; others add tomato, garlic, chile, or a little stock.

replace_steps.title: Boil water
replace_steps.instruction: Bring water and salt to a strong boil in a heavy pot.

replace_steps.title: Cook ugali
replace_steps.instruction: Add maize flour gradually while stirring hard. Keep folding and pressing until the ugali is firm and pulls from the sides.

replace_steps.title: Shape ugali
replace_steps.instruction: Turn onto a wet plate and shape into a dome with a wet spoon. Cover to keep warm.

replace_steps.title: Start greens
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onion until soft. Add garlic if using, then tomatoes, and cook until they break down.

replace_steps.title: Cook sukuma
replace_steps.instruction: Add sliced greens and chile if using. Toss until coated, then cover briefly until wilted but still bright.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Season the greens and serve hot beside the ugali.

recipes.review_summary: The old motoho sequence cooked the porridge before fermentation, which is not the strongest fit for sour sorghum porridge. The corrected version ferments a sorghum slurry first, then cooks it into a smooth breakfast porridge.
recommended_changes: Ferment sorghum flour slurry overnight before cooking.
Use sorghum malt or a spoon of previous motoho as starter when available.
Make optional sugar a serving choice, not the base flavor.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Food science and Basotho food sources describe motoho as fermented sorghum porridge or drink, with a sour slurry cooked into porridge.

sources.title: World Food Map - Fermented Sorghum Porridge

recipe.description: Basotho sour sorghum porridge made by fermenting a sorghum slurry overnight, then cooking it smooth for breakfast.
recipe.story: Motoho needs time more than fuss. Let the sorghum sour gently overnight, then cook it slowly until the raw edge disappears.
recipe.best_served_with: Plain, lightly sweetened, or with milk offered separately
recipe.regional_variations: Some households back-slop with a little previous motoho; others use sorghum malt or natural fermentation.

replace_ingredients.name: sorghum malt or previous motoho starter

replace_steps.title: Make slurry
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk sorghum flour with lukewarm water until smooth. Stir in sorghum malt or starter if using.

replace_steps.title: Ferment
replace_steps.instruction: Cover loosely and leave in a warm place until pleasantly sour and lightly bubbly.

replace_steps.title: Cook porridge
replace_steps.instruction: Bring the remaining water to a boil. Whisk in the fermented slurry slowly and reduce the heat.

replace_steps.title: Simmer smooth
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer, stirring often, until the porridge is smooth and the raw sorghum taste is gone.

replace_steps.title: Finish
replace_steps.instruction: Season lightly with salt or sugar if using. Serve warm, or cool to drink as a thinner sour porridge.

recipes.review_summary: The old nyekoe leaned toward samp and beans, but stronger Basotho references point to beans, sorghum, and pumpkin as the more distinctive nyekoe profile. The corrected version uses that highland combination.
recommended_changes: Use sorghum grain, dried beans, and pumpkin.
Keep the dish thick and gently mashed.
Let soaking and slow simmering do the work.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Lesotho food references consistently describe nyekoe as a beans, sorghum, and pumpkin or grain stew.

sources.title: World Food and Wine - Food in Lesotho
Sunheron - What to Eat in Lesotho

recipe.description: Basotho bean, sorghum, and pumpkin stew simmered slowly until thick, earthy, and gently sweet.
recipe.story: Nyekoe is patient mountain food. Soak the beans, simmer the sorghum, and add pumpkin late so it softens without disappearing completely.
recipe.best_served_with: Papa, moroho, chile relish, or sour milk on the side
recipe.regional_variations: Some homes use samp or cracked maize in place of sorghum; pumpkin or potatoes are common additions for sweetness and body.

replace_steps.title: Soak beans
replace_steps.instruction: Cover beans with plenty of water and soak overnight. Drain before cooking.

replace_steps.title: Start grains
replace_steps.instruction: Combine beans, sorghum, onion, oil, and water in a heavy pot. Bring to a boil.

replace_steps.title: Simmer slowly
replace_steps.instruction: Reduce heat and simmer until the beans and sorghum are nearly tender, adding water as needed.

replace_steps.title: Add pumpkin
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in pumpkin and continue cooking until it is soft and the stew is thick.

replace_steps.title: Season and mash
replace_steps.instruction: Season with salt and pepper. Mash a small portion against the pot to make the stew creamy.

recipes.review_summary: The existing papa le moroho recipe was close. The update keeps the everyday Basotho structure while improving water ratios, seasoning, and the greens method.
recommended_changes: Keep papa as stiff maize meal porridge.
Cook moroho as leafy greens with onion, tomato, oil, and salt.
Avoid overcomplicating a simple daily plate.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Aid, food, and cultural references consistently describe papa with cooked greens as a staple Basotho meal.

sources.title: Help Lesotho - How to Make Papa
FAO Lesotho Home Gardening Poster - Papa le Moroho
World Food and Wine - Food in Lesotho

recipe.description: Lesotho's everyday plate of firm maize papa served with moroho, leafy greens cooked with onion, tomato, oil, and salt.
recipe.story: Papa le moroho is simple by design. Make the papa firm enough to scoop and keep the greens tender, seasoned, and generous.
recipe.best_served_with: Beans, stewed meat, eggs, or tomato relish
recipe.regional_variations: Moroho may be wild greens, spinach, chard, or cabbage depending on season and household.

replace_steps.title: Start papa
replace_steps.instruction: Bring most of the water to a boil. Mix a little maize meal with cool water to make a slurry, then stir it into the pot.

replace_steps.title: Thicken papa
replace_steps.instruction: Add the remaining maize meal gradually, stirring hard until very thick and pulling from the pot.

replace_steps.title: Steam papa
replace_steps.instruction: Cover and steam the papa on low heat so the meal cooks through.

replace_steps.title: Cook tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Cook onion until soft, then add tomatoes and salt and cook until saucy.

replace_steps.title: Cook moroho
replace_steps.instruction: Add chopped greens and cook until tender. Add a splash of water only if the pan dries out.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Serve a mound of papa with moroho alongside or spooned over the top.

recipes.review_summary: The old Liberian jollof was missing several common aromatics and vegetables. The corrected version keeps the Liberian mixed-protein feel while adding bell pepper, garlic, stock, thyme, bay, carrot, green beans, and cabbage.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, bell pepper, stock, thyme, bay leaf, and mixed vegetables.
Remove halal because smoked meats and sourcing vary.
Keep fried plantain and pepper sauce as serving notes.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian jollof sources support meat or seafood, tomato paste, tomatoes, onions, peppers, stock, vegetables, and thyme.

recipe.description: Liberian jollof rice with chicken, smoked turkey, tomato stew, bell pepper, stock, thyme, bay, and mixed vegetables.
recipe.story: Liberian jollof is generous. The rice should taste of tomato stew and meat stock, with vegetables folded through the pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Fried plantain, pepper sauce, boiled egg, or cabbage salad
recipe.regional_variations: Proteins vary by home: chicken, smoked turkey, beef, shrimp, fish, or a meatless vegetable version.

replace_steps.title: Cook protein
replace_steps.instruction: Season chicken with salt and pepper, simmer until nearly tender, and reserve the stock. Add smoked turkey or fish later so it does not break apart.

replace_steps.title: Fry tomato base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat oil and cook onions and bell pepper until soft. Add garlic and tomato paste and fry until the paste darkens.

replace_steps.title: Cook stew
replace_steps.instruction: Add tomatoes, thyme, bay leaves, chile if using, and some stock. Simmer until the sauce thickens.

replace_steps.title: Add rice
replace_steps.instruction: Stir rice into the sauce, then add chicken, smoked protein, vegetables, and enough stock to cook the rice.

replace_steps.title: Steam rice
replace_steps.instruction: Cover tightly and cook on low until the rice is tender and the liquid is absorbed.

replace_steps.title: Rest and serve
replace_steps.instruction: Rest covered, fluff gently, and serve with fried plantain and pepper sauce.

recipes.review_summary: The old palm butter soup captured the main idea but needed stronger seasoning and liquid guidance. The corrected version makes palm fruit concentrate, meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, onion, and okra or bitterball cook as a coherent Liberian soup.
recommended_changes: Add garlic, dried shrimp or crayfish, stock or water, and salt.
Simmer until the palm oil rises and the soup thickens.
Keep rice or fufu as the main pairing.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian sources consistently describe palm butter soup as palm fruit base simmered with meat, fish, seafood, chile, and rice or fufu.

sources.title: African Food Recipes - Liberian Palm Butter Soup
Recipe Buster - Liberian Palm Butter Soup
Peace Corps Cooking - Liberia Palm Butter Soup

recipe.description: Liberian palm butter soup with palm fruit concentrate, beef or goat, smoked fish, dried shrimp, chile, okra, and rice.
recipe.story: Palm butter soup should be deep orange, glossy, and rich. Give the palm base time to simmer until the oil rises and the smoke from the fish seasons the whole pot.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, dumboy, or boiled cassava
recipe.regional_variations: Fresh palm nuts make the deepest flavor, but palm fruit concentrate is the practical home shortcut.

replace_steps.title: Cook meat
replace_steps.instruction: Season meat with salt, pepper, onion, and garlic. Simmer with water until nearly tender, then reserve the stock.

replace_steps.title: Start palm base
replace_steps.instruction: Whisk palm fruit concentrate with stock in a large pot and bring to a simmer.

replace_steps.title: Add proteins
replace_steps.instruction: Add cooked meat, smoked fish, dried shrimp if using, onions, garlic, and peppers.

replace_steps.title: Simmer glossy
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer until the soup thickens and red oil rises to the surface. Stir occasionally so it does not catch.

replace_steps.title: Finish vegetables
replace_steps.instruction: Add okra or bitterball if using and cook until tender. Adjust salt and serve over rice.

recipes.review_summary: The old torborgee page overfocused on locust beans and did not reflect the better documented Liberian beans or bitter vegetable torborgee pattern. The corrected version makes it a thick palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, hot pepper, and rice.
recommended_changes: Use red cowpeas or lentils as the base, with bitter eggplant when available.
Keep palm oil, dried shrimp or fish, onion, garlic, and chile.
Remove pescatarian because meat or mixed proteins are common.
recipes.official_source_status: No official recipe standard found. Liberian food sources describe torborgee as a thick, spicy, palm-oily stew associated with beans, bitter vegetables, dried proteins, and rice.

sources.title: The Closing Argument - Popular Liberian Dishes

recipe.description: Liberian torborgee, a fiery palm-oil stew of red cowpeas or lentils, bitter eggplant, dried seafood, onion, garlic, and chile.
recipe.story: Torborgee is not shy food. It should be thick, red with palm oil, bitter around the edges, and hot enough that plain rice becomes necessary.
recipe.best_served_with: White rice, fufu, or boiled cassava
recipe.regional_variations: Some versions use red cowpeas, lentils, or African pea eggplants; proteins may include dried fish, shrimp, chicken, smoked meat, or no meat.

replace_ingredients.name: African pea eggplant or bitter eggplant

replace_steps.title: Cook beans
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer cowpeas or lentils in water until tender but not falling apart. Reserve some cooking liquid.

replace_steps.title: Build palm base
replace_steps.instruction: Heat palm oil and cook onions until soft. Add garlic and peppers and stir until fragrant.

replace_steps.title: Add bitter vegetable
replace_steps.instruction: Add bitter eggplant if using and cook until it begins to soften.

replace_steps.title: Add proteins and beans
replace_steps.instruction: Stir in dried fish, dried shrimp if using, cooked beans, and enough cooking liquid for a thick stew.

replace_steps.title: Simmer thick
replace_steps.instruction: Simmer until the palm oil colors the stew deeply and the texture is thick enough to spoon over rice.

replace_steps.title: Serve
replace_steps.instruction: Adjust salt and pepper. Serve in a small, intense portion over plenty of white rice.
